greeting_etiquette.title: Greeting Etiquette
tips: Use 'Buongiorno' (morning/afternoon) and 'Buonasera' (evening) with strangers and in formal situations; 'Ciao' is informal and used with friends.
Use 'Lei' (formal you) with strangers and older people; 'tu' is for friends and peers — switching to 'tu' is usually explicitly proposed.
A handshake is standard in formal introductions; among friends, expect two kisses on the cheeks (left first, then right).
When entering a small shop or office, greet everyone with a general 'Buongiorno'; silence is considered impolite.
Saying 'Arrivederci' (formal) or 'Ciao' (informal) when leaving is expected — never leave without a goodbye.

restaurant_customs.title: Restaurant Customs
tips: Italian meals follow a traditional course structure: antipasto, primo (pasta/rice), secondo (meat/fish), contorno (side), dolce (dessert) — but ordering only one or two courses is perfectly acceptable.
The 'coperto' (cover charge) on the bill is standard and covers bread and table service — it is not a tip.
Tipping ('mancia') is not expected but appreciated; rounding up the bill or leaving 1–2 euros is common.
Never ask for parmesan cheese on seafood pasta — it is considered a culinary faux pas.
Dinner service starts late by many standards: 7:30–9:00 PM is typical; restaurants may not open before 7:00 PM.

shopping_norms.title: Shopping Norms
tips: Many small shops close for a lunch break ('pausa pranzo') from approximately 1:00 PM to 3:30 PM.
Sales seasons ('saldi') are regulated by regional law and typically run in January/February and July/August.
Always greet the shopkeeper when entering ('Buongiorno') and say goodbye when leaving ('Arrivederci').
Haggling is not customary in shops but may be acceptable at outdoor markets and antique fairs.
Sunday and Monday mornings are common closing times for small and family-run shops.

transit_tips.title: Transit Tips
tips: Regional train tickets must be validated ('convalidare') in the small green machines on the platform before boarding — failing to do so risks a fine.
Italy has two main train operators: Trenitalia and Italo (NTV) — compare prices as they can differ significantly.
City buses and trams require pre-purchased tickets, available at 'tabaccherie' (tobacco shops) or newsstands — validate on board.
Strikes ('scioperi') are relatively common — check transport authority websites for announcements.
High-speed trains (Frecciarossa, Frecciargento) have assigned seats; regional trains generally do not.

pharmacy_expectations.title: Pharmacy Expectations
tips: Italian pharmacists are highly qualified professionals who can recommend treatments for minor health issues.
Pharmacies ('farmacie') are marked by a green cross; 'parafarmacie' sell a smaller range of non-prescription items.
A duty pharmacy ('farmacia di turno') schedule is posted on every pharmacy door — there is always one open nearby at night and on holidays.
Bring your passport or European Health Insurance Card if you need a prescription filled as a foreigner.

general_social_norms.title: General Social Norms
tips: Attempting to speak Italian, even imperfectly, is warmly appreciated — Italians are generally patient and encouraging with language learners.
Food culture is deeply important — avoid asking for ketchup, requesting major menu modifications, or ordering cappuccino after a meal.
Personal space is smaller than in many Northern European or North American cultures — expect animated gestures and closer conversation distance.
Italians tend to dress well even for casual outings; wearing beachwear or very casual clothing away from the beach may attract attention.
Meals are a social occasion and should not be rushed — lunchtime is sacred and dinner can last two hours or more.
